title: The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands v Sean Sullivan (costs) (CL 53 of 2020) [2026] TCASC 14 (19 February 2026)
text:
I am
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF THE ACTION NO. CL-53/20
TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
BETWEEN:
THE HONOURABLE ATTORNEY GENERAL PLAINTIFF
OF THE TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
-and-
SEAN SULLIVAN DEFENDANT
Before: The Hon. Mr Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Appearances: Mr Laurence Harris of Cooley (UK) LLP and Ms Clemar
Hippolyte of the Attorney General’s Chambers for the Plaintiff
Mr Conrad Griffiths KC and Mr Devonte Smith of Dentons TCI
(formerly Griffiths & Partners) for the Defendant
Hearing Date: 7 January 2026.
Venue: Court 1, Supreme Court, Leeward Highway, Providenciales.
DECISION ON COSTS
1. On 10 November 2025, I handed down the judgment in this matter, dismissing the claim
and stating that I would hear from counsel on the issue of costs.
2. The matter came back before me on 7 January 2026, where I heard submissions on that
issue and reserved my decision to consider the case law presented.
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 1 of 20

3. The parties agree that the general principle is that costs should follow the event, i.e. the
winner pays the loser's costs.
4. Order 62 r.3(3) of the Civil Rules 2000 provides:
If the Court in the exercise of its discretion sees fit to make any order as to the
cost of any proceedings, the Court shall order the costs to follow the event, except
when it appears to the Court that in the circumstances of the case some other order
should be made as to the whole or any part of the costs.
5. Note 62/3/3 in the Supreme Court Practice 19991 provides:
“In exercising the discretion over costs, a judge should be guided by the following
principles. In the case of a wholly successful defendant the judge must award him
costs unless there is evidence:
(1) That the defendant brought about the litigation; or
(2) has done something connected with the institution or the conduct of the
suit calculated to occasion unnecessary litigation and expense; or
(3) has done some wrongful act in the course of the transaction of which the
plaintiff complains.”
6. Mr Harris’ opening position was that whilst accepting that the claim had been dismissed,
the Plaintiff had succeeded on certain issues on which substantial time and costs had been
incurred and that the conduct of the Defendant in the litigation had been such that there
should be a substantial reduction in the costs which the Defendant should be entitled to
recover. Mr Harris refers to Ord. 62 r.10, which provides:
Where it appears to the Court in any proceedings that any thing has been done, or
that omission has been made, unreasonably or improperly by or on behalf of any
party, the Court may order that the costs of that party in respect of the act or
omission, as the case may be, shall not be allowed and that any costs occasioned
by it to any other party shall be paid by him to that other party.
7. What Mr Harris urges me to do is to make an ‘issue-based cost order’ which he suggests
1 England and Wales – ‘The White Book’.
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 2 of 20

should be:
“i. The Plaintiff shall pay the Defendant’s costs of the claim, to be assessed on
the standard basis if not agreed, on the issues of: (a) whether the Defendant
assisted Minister Hanchell’s breach of fiduciary duty; and (b) whether the
Plaintiff suffered any loss;
ii. The Defendant shall pay the Plaintiff’s costs of the claim, to be assessed on
the standard basis if not agreed, on the issues of: (a) the admissibility of the
Plaintiff’s Civil Evidence Act notices; (b) whether Minister Hanchell breached
his fiduciary duty; and (c) the Defendant’s additional arguments which were not
addressed in the Court’s judgment, namely: (i) the alleged failure of the
Plaintiff to call oral witnesses and any adverse inferences said to arise
therefrom; (ii) that the Court ought not to pierce the corporate veil; (iii) that
Cooley acted in the proceedings without a business licence; (iv) that Plaintiff’s
Counsel, Mr Harris, was not properly admitted and (v) that the Attorney
General ought not to have instructed Cooley to conduct civil recovery claims;
iii. There shall be no order as to costs: (a) on the issue of whether the
Defendant was dishonest; and (b) in relation to the Defendant’s equitable
defences.
8. He then puts forward an alternative broad-brush approach to consider what proportion of
the Defendant’s costs should be recoverable, having regard to the outcome of different
issues. He suggests that if this approach is adopted, the Defendant should be deprived of
75% of his costs.
9. Mr Harris breaks the issues which the Court had to decide into seven headings:
a) Issues of admissibility of the Plaintiff’s Civil Evidence Act notices raised by the
Defendant;
b) Breach of fiduciary duty;
c) Assistance;
d) Dishonesty;
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 3 of 20

e) The equitable defences;
f) Loss; and
g) Sundry issues.
and suggests that the Defendant prevailed on only 2 issues: assistance and loss.
10. On the question of whether an issue-based costs order would be appropriate, Mr Griffiths
KC directs me to Re Elgindata (No. 2),2 an authority adopted by Mr Harris in his oral
submissions as the appropriate statement as to the principles the Court should apply.
11. Elgindata was an appeal by the successful petitioners against the trial judge’s order on
costs. The preamble reads:
“The petitioners were successful in obtaining an order under s 459 of the
Companies Act 1985 that P, another shareholder in the company, purchase their
shares. The judge found that for the most part the petitioners' case had failed but
that there was some conduct on the part of P that constituted unfairly prejudicial
conduct and ordered, inter alia, that three-quarters of P's costs should be paid
by the petitioners and one-quarter of the petitioners' costs should be paid by P.
The petitioners' costs were estimated at £120,000 and those of P at £200,000 so
that the petitioners would have to bear costs of some £240,000.” (Emphasis
added)
12. As Nourse LJ sets out in the leading judgment:3
“In this instance we have had to investigate and identify the principles on which
a successful party may be deprived of costs or may even be ordered to pay costs
to the unsuccessful party.
…
If they were to be taxed in those amounts, the result would be that the petitioners,
albeit that they succeeded in obtaining the relief which they sought, would have
to bear total costs of £240,000 against a total of £80,000 borne by Mr Purslow.
The price to be paid by Mr Purslow for the petitioners' shares has now been fixed
2 [1993] 1 All ER 232; [1992] 1 W.L.R. 1207.
3 At [233] j.
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 4 of 20

by agreement at no more than £24,600. These figures are enough to show that, if
the judge's order for costs stands, the petitioners' victory will, as Beldam LJ
observed in argument, have become worse than pyrrhic. In purely monetary terms
it will have proved to be an annihilating defeat. I should make it clear that none
of the figures were before the judge when he gave his decision on costs and that
they cannot have any influence on anything which we have to decide on this
appeal. I refer to them only in order to demonstrate the great importance of our
decision to the parties in the suit.”
13. Mr Harris referred me to the headnote, which Mr Griffiths KC extracts in his skeleton
argument. Accordingly, it is appropriate to repeat the same:
“The principles on which costs were to be awarded were (i) that costs were in the
discretion of the court, (ii) that costs should follow the event except when it
appeared to the court that in the circumstances of the case some other order
should be made, (iii) that the general rule did not cease to apply simply because
the successful party raised issues or made allegations that failed, but that he
could be deprived of his costs in whole or in part where he had caused a
significant increase in the length of the proceedings, and (iv) that where the
successful party raised issues or made allegations improperly or unreasonably
the court could not only deprive him of his costs but could also order him to pay
the whole or part of the unsuccessful party's costs. The fourth principle implied,
moreover, that a successful party who neither improperly nor unreasonably
raised issues or made allegations which failed ought not to be ordered to pay any
part of the unsuccessful party's costs…” (My emphasis)
14. Whilst Elgindata is the most helpful authority, it being a pre-CPR4 case, Mr Griffiths KC
also referred me to Pigot -v- The Environment Agency5 in which Stephen Jourdan KC,
sitting as a High Court judge, referred to CPR r.44.2(7) which provides:
(7) Before the court considers making an order under paragraph (6)(f), it will
consider whether it is practicable to make an order under paragraph (6)(a) or (c)
4 Civil Procedure Rules 1998 – England and Wales.
5 [2020] EWHC (Ch).
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 5 of 20

instead.”
15. Paragraphs (6)(a), (c) and (f) provide:
(6) The orders which the court may make under this rule include an order that the
party must pay -
(a) a proportion of the other party’s costs;
(c) costs from or until a certain date only;
(f) costs relating to a distinct part of the proceedings.
16. The learned judge summarised the principles which guide the Court in applying the above
rules where one party has succeeded overall, but has lost on one or more issues, and the
unsuccessful party overall is seeking, or the Court is considering making, an issue-based
costs order, as follows:
“(1) The mere fact that the successful party was not successful on every issue
does not, of itself, justify an issue-based cost order. In any litigation, there are
likely to be issues which involve reviewing the same, or overlapping, sets of facts,
and where it is therefore difficult to disentangle the costs of one issue from
another. The mere fact that the successful party has lost on one or more issues
does not by itself normally make it appropriate to deprive them of their costs.
(2) Such an order may be appropriate if there is a discrete or distinct issue, the
raising of which caused additional costs to be incurred. Such an order may also
be appropriate if the overall costs were materially increased by the unreasonable
raising of one or more issues on which the successful party failed.
(3) Where there is a discrete issue which caused additional costs to be incurred,
if the issue was raised reasonably, the successful party is likely to be deprived of
its costs of the issue. If the issue was raised unreasonably, the successful party is
likely also to be ordered to pay the costs of the issue incurred by the unsuccessful
party. An issue may be treated as having been raised unreasonably if it is hopeless
and ought never to have been pursued.
(4) Where an issue based costs order is appropriate, the court should attempt to
reflect it by ordering payment of a proportion of the receiving party's costs if that
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 6 of 20

is practicable.
(5) An issue based costs order should reflect the extent to which the costs were
increased by the raising of the issue; costs which would have been incurred even
if the issue had not been raised should be paid by the unsuccessful party.
(6) Before making an issue-based costs order, it is important to stand back and
ask whether, applying the principles set out in CPR r.44.2, it is in all the
circumstances of the case the right result. The aim must always be to make an
order that reflects the overall justice of the case. (Emphasis added)
17. In Elgindata, Nourse LJ observed that out of the authorities to which the court had been
referred, it was only in two of them that there were observations which bore directly on the
principles with respect to issue-based costs orders,6 and stated “That no doubt is because
being axiomatic, it is only rarely that they rise to the surface of judicial expression.”
(Emphasis added)
18. He then references a first instance decision of his own in Re Bird Precision Bellows Ltd7
citing8 that he had made “an erroneous supposition that the outcome of the valuation
dispute had been a draw”, and he deprived the successful party of 15% of their costs.
19. He said:
“One of my reasons for referring to that decision is that it is another example of
a case where the trial judge had overlooked or failed to apply basic principles as
to costs … But I refer to it primarily for some observations of Oliver LJ, which
expressly supports the third of the principles I have stated and impliedly supports
the fourth. Having expressed the view that it was clearly a case in which on any
normal view the petitioners would have been entitled to their costs of proving
6 The principles to which he refers are: (1) Costs are in the discretion of the court. (2) They should follow the event,
except when it appears to the court that in the circumstances of the case some other order should be made. (3)
The general rule does not cease to apply simply because the successful party raises issues or makes allegations on
which he fails, but where that has caused a significant increase in the length or cost of proceedings, he may be
deprived of the whole or part of his costs. (4) Where the successful party raises issues or makes allegations
improperly or unreasonably, the court may not only deprive him of the costs but order him to pay the whole or a
part of the unsuccessful party’s costs.
7 [1984] 3 All ER 444, [1984] Ch 419 affd [1985] 3All ER 523, [1986] CH 658.
8 At [238] b.
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 7 of 20

their case, Oliver LJ continued:
‘I can see a ground, if the learned judge had been of this view, for
depriving them of perhaps part of their costs of the valuation on the
footing that the evidence as to valuation had been so extreme that it had
wasted part of the time or something of that sort. But that is not the case
and he has treated the matter simply on the basis that there was a draw
and decided to deprive the respondents of all their costs of the valuation.
That, as it seems to me, must on the stated reason that there was a draw
be wrong. It was not a draw. It was a victory for the [petitioners]. In those
circumstances it seems to me that the discretion was plainly wrongly
exercised in this case and in my judgment the [petitioners] are entitled to
the whole of the costs of the hearing below.’ (Emphasis added)
20. In Ritter and Wife -v- Godfrey9 cited with approval in Capron -v- Government of the
Turks and Caicos Islands10 Lord Sterndale MR held:
“But there is such a settled practice of the Courts that in the absence of special
circumstances a successful litigant should receive his costs, that it is necessary
to show some ground for exercising a discretion by refusing an order which
would give them to him. The discretion must be judicially exercised, and therefore
there must be some grounds for its exercise, for a discretion exercised on no
grounds cannot be judicial. If, however, there be any grounds, the question of
whether they are sufficient is entirely for the judge at the trial, and this Court
cannot interfere with his discretion.
…
One other consideration applies to these cases. The principle as to the exercise
of discretion is the same in the case of plaintiffs and defendants, but it is clear
that considerations sufficient to justify a refusal of costs to a plaintiff are not
necessarily sufficient in the case of a defendant, for the former initiates the
litigation while the latter is brought into it against his will.11
9 [1918-19] All ER Rep 714; [1920] 2 KB 47.
10 [2010] UKPC 2.
11 See para. 40 below.
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 8 of 20

Speaking generally, I think it may be said that, in order to justify an order refusing
a defendant his costs, he must be shown to have been guilty of conduct which
induced the plaintiff to bring the action, and without which it would probably not
have been brought. This is so stated by Vaughan Williams L.J. in Bostock v.
Ramsey Urban Council (1), and it generally may be tested by the question stated
in the judgment of the two other members of the Court, A. L. Smith L.J. and Romer
L.J. in the same case, i.e., was the defendant's conduct such as to encourage the
plaintiff to believe that he had a good cause of action?
I do not say that this is the only test, but I think it is the one properly applied to
this case. (Emphasis added)
21. In Capron, the Board held:
“43. The trial judge in this case found certain aspects of the actions (and
inaction) of TCInvest and the Department of Lands and Survey to be
“disgraceful”. We make no comment on this finding beyond saying that none of
the behaviour of which the judge complained comes within the categories
adumbrated in Ritter. Significantly, Martin J accepted that the actions which he
condemned were either deliberate or the result of gross incompetence. If, as the
judge acknowledged, they might have been the result of ineptitude, that
conclusion would be plainly incompatible with the view that the respondent had
been in fact responsible for any of the species of cases referred to in Ritter which
would justify an award of costs against it. (Emphasis added)
44. … We consider that the Court of Appeal was therefore correct in its decision
to reverse the trial judge's finding on the costs issue.”
22. I am not of the view that this is a matter that lends itself to an issue-based costs order in the
sense that the Defendant should pay some of the Plaintiff’s costs. As such, the question
that arises is whether the Defendant should be deprived of any of his costs.
23. In Antonelli -v- Allen and another12 it was held that in deciding on the question of costs
in a case where there were several issues on which the successful party in the action did
12 [2000] All ER (D) 2570.
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 9 of 20

not succeed, the court should stand back and consider the matter globally, as to the extent
to which it was just in all the circumstances to deprive the successful party of all or any of
its costs because of the fact that it was unsuccessful on one or more points.
24. Mr Harris submits:
“While the Defendant won because the Plaintiff failed to establish assistance
which was a necessary element of the claim, the Defendant did not win on all
issues. In fact, the Defendant only won on two issues (assistance and loss) and it
is therefore necessary to consider the impact of the other issues in relation to the
costs incurred, and in particular, how reasonable it was to have taken those
issues, what the outcome on each of them was and how that should be reflected
in costs.”
Dishonest Assistance
25. As set out in paragraph 9 above, Mr Harris breaks the elements of ‘dishonest assistance’
into individual issues on which he suggests that the Court should consider who won on
each issue and award costs accordingly. I do not agree with that analysis. For the Plaintiff
to succeed, it had to prove all the elements of the tort, that is:
a) The existence of a trust or fiduciary duty;
b) Breach of trust or fiduciary duty;
c) That the Defendant assisted in that breach; and
d) In doing so, acted dishonestly.
26. If there is a failure to prove any one of those elements, then the claim fails. As Mr Griffiths
KC puts it:
“These proceedings always concerned a singular claim (albeit with several
component parts) and the outcome on the dishonest assistance claim was always
going to be either that the Plaintiff was successful in making out dishonest
assistance or that it was unsuccessful. In this case, the latter was the result so
costs should follow the event.”
27. On the substantive claim, there is only one question: did the Plaintiff discharge the burden
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 10 of 20

of proving the claim? As Mr Griffiths KC aptly puts it, the issue is binary. The answer to
that question is ‘No’.
28. Whilst I do not agree with Mr Harris’ approach that each of the separate essential elements
should be considered as discrete or distinct issues, I will address them on that basis, as that
is how it has been argued.
29. It was not disputed that the Minister owed a fiduciary duty to his principal and also that I
found a breach of that duty; however, the claim failed as the Plaintiff did not satisfy the
Court that the Defendant had assisted in the breach. It cannot be said that the issue of
whether there had been a breach of duty was not something the Defendant was entitled to
challenge. It was perfectly reasonable for him to do so, as it was an element of the tort
which the Plaintiff had to prove, and was not a discrete or distinct issue.
30. Mr Harris submits that it was unreasonable for the Defendant to have argued the issue,
“particularly in the light of previous judgments of the court determining that Mr Hanchell
was in breach of his fiduciary duty in very similar circumstances.”13 In consequence, he
says that the Defendant should pay the costs of dealing with that issue, underpinning his
suggestion that the Court should make an issue-based order, in the sense of the Defendant
paying the Plaintiff’s costs on the issues ‘lost’, and discounting or ignoring principle four
in paragraph 16 above.
31. I disagree, and the suggestion that the Defendant should have simply capitulated overlooks
that I found that in the authorities relied on by the Plaintiff, the circumstances of the breach
of duty were distinguishable.
32. With respect to the required element of ‘dishonesty’, Mr Harris submits that as the Court
made no findings one way or the other, neither party can be said to have won that issue,
and as such, the Defendant should not have his costs of dealing with it.
33. There was no requirement for me to make any findings with respect to ‘dishonesty’ unless
I was required to deal with the equitable defences raised by the Defendant (to which I return
below), as the claim had already failed with respect to ‘assistance’. In the event, much of
13 See AG -v- Arthur [CL 118/10]; AG -v- Robinson [CL 178/10] and AG -v- Akita Holdings Limited [CL 148/11] and
[Civil Appeal 23 of 2013].
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 11 of 20

the argument with respect to dishonesty was not, at trial, directed to the element of the tort,
but to whether the Defendant was entitled to rely on equitable defences due to his (wider)
alleged dishonesty, i.e. had he come to equity with ‘clean hands’. Putting that to one side,
the approach suggested by Mr Harris with respect to the ‘dishonesty’ element appears to
be importing into the cost consideration that the winning party must win every point in
order to recover. That, in my view, is plainly wrong on the authorities cited above. In
concluding that the Defendant should not be deprived of his costs on the ‘dishonesty’
element, I adopt the thinking of Stephen Jourdan KC in Pigot, where, in refusing to deny
the plaintiff his costs, he says:
“I made no determination as to whether the potential ability of the Defendant to
secure a variation of the conditions of the Claimant’s licence under the Water
Resources Act 1991 may affect any relief to which the Claimant is entitled. The
Claimant did not lose that issue.” (My emphasis)
34. There is, in my view, nothing in the Defendant’s approach to the claim of dishonest
assistance that would give rise to moving away from the general principle that the overall
winning party should have his costs.
35. The matter does not rest there. As set out in paragraph 9 above, other issues have been
raised by the Plaintiff, which Mr Harris suggests should deprive the Defendant of some of
his costs or which costs should be paid by the Defendant. The question, then, is whether
these amount to discrete or distinct issues upon which the Defendant can be said to have
lost, the raising of which caused additional costs to be incurred.
Equitable Defences
36. The Defendant’s case on this issue was, in essence, that he had expended considerable
sums of money in predevelopment costs, in reliance on representations made to him by
successive administrations of the Government of the Turks and Caicos Islands, that his
development project(s) would be approved and as such, it was unconscionable and/or
inequitable to bring this claim against him, particularly in circumstances where the claim
had lapsed for over 11 years before commencement.
37. Mr Harris submits that the equitable defences took up a very significant amount of time in
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 12 of 20

cross-examination and written and oral submissions. He then submits that the Court
declined to reach any finding on the equitable defences, one way or the other. He then
asserts that if the Court had thought there was any merit in the defences, then it would have
dealt with them in the judgment.
38. It is not uncommon for the Court not to deal with certain issues in a judgment when there
is no need to do so,14 as the matter has been decided, as in this case, on the basis that the
Plaintiff failed to discharge its burden of proof. Put simply, in a debt claim, the court need
not consider a defence that the debt has been paid, until the plaintiff has established that a
debt is due.
39. The Defendant did not lose on the equitable defences, and I refer again to the approach
taken in Pigot. Had a case of dishonest assistance been made out, then these defences
would have been proper issues to raise, as on the evidence, it cannot be gainsaid that the
Defendant was encouraged to pursue his endeavours.15
40. I am also mindful that this is a case where the unsuccessful party is the Plaintiff, not a
defendant who is arguing he should not have to pay the costs, as in the circumstances of
the authorities cited. The Defendant was entitled to put forward his best possible case16 in
the face of a claim amounting to in excess of $20,000,000 when compound interest is taken
into account. It was clearly not an insignificant claim that he faced, and he would want to
put forward a robust defence.
41. Amec Engineering -v- Norwich Union17 is illustrative of when a winning party may be
deprived of its costs or made to pay the costs of the unsuccessful issue. The court in
dismissing the claim, concluded:
“For the reasons which I have given this action fails and is dismissed. However,
14 See Pigot at para. 33 above.
15 Whether that encouragement would have been sufficient to amount to a defence was not something that I
needed to consider for the reasons I have already said, but see Capron -v- Government Of Turks And Caicos Islands
[2010] UKPC 2.
16 I was referred by Mr Griffiths KC to Kew -v- Bettamix Ltd and Others [2007] 4 Costs 527; [2006] EWCA Civ 1535.
17 [2003] EWHC 1341 (TCC); [2003] All ER (D) 158 (Jun), in which Leverson LJ said at para. 49 “He argues that this
case endorses the principle that a claimant is entitled to put his case at its highest. I entirely accept that
proposition. On the other hand, however, there is a distinction between putting the case at its highest (for
example, in relation to many heads of damage in a personal injuries claim) and advancing a basis for relief on a
basis that fails (especially if it is entirely unsupportable).
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 13 of 20

for the reasons which I have also given it seems to me that it may be appropriate
to consider carefully by which party the costs of this action, or at any rate the
bulk of them, should be paid, bearing in mind that AMEC has been successful in
vindicating its reputation as a civil engineering contractor in the face of a
sustained, but wholly groundless, attack with which virtually the whole of the
action and the trial have been concerned.
42. In Amec, a claim was made under a contractor's all-risk insurance policy, which Norwich
Union had rejected. The claim was defended on two bases. Firstly, that the claim fell
outside the provisions of the policy in that the subject matter was not ‘insured property’,
which the court readily accepted, and secondly, that the policy did not cover “loss of or
damage to and the costs necessary to replace, repair or rectify that part of the permanent
or temporary works which is in a defective condition due to a negligent design…”
(Emphasis added)
43. The equitable defences do not fall into the category of being wholly groundless, and as I
have noted above in relation to dishonesty, much of the argument as to the Defendant’s
alleged dishonesty went to the issue of whether he could rely on equitable defences at all.
I do not see any good reason to deprive the Defendant of his costs of this issue.
Loss
44. With respect to the issue of loss, Mr Harris concedes that it is fair to say that the Plaintiff
lost on that issue. Given that, it is difficult to see why it has been raised in the costs
arguments; however, Mr Harris suggests that the fact that the Plaintiff could have elected
for an account of profit in the alternative has some impact. I do not see the argument, and
certainly not to the extent that such an issue should give rise to a reduction in the costs
recoverable, in circumstances where he specifically requested that the Court assess any
equitable compensation and where, it seems to me, an account of profit would not be
desirable.
Sundry Issues
45. Under this heading, Mr Harris raises the following additional issues:
a) Plaintiff’s failure to call any witnesses and the adverse inference that should be
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 14 of 20

drawn;
b) The validity of the Civil Evidence Act notices;
c) The argument that the Plaintiff was seeking to pierce the corporate veil;
d) The allegation that Cooley (UK) LLP is acting without the benefit of any business
licence in the Turks and Caicos Islands;
e) Whether Mr Harris should be disqualified from conducting the matter;
46. Mr Harris again runs the same argument, that as these issues did not feature in the
judgment, then the Defendant lost on them. I do not need to repeat that I do not think that
is a correct approach. Save for a) and b) above, these issues did not take up any significant
time or increase in costs that cannot be addressed by taxation and were not separate,
discrete and distinct issues. They arose (where they did arise) from the overall conduct of
this litigation. The issues raised in d) and e) were not issues at trial.18
47. The one issue that does invite consideration is the challenge to the Plaintiff’s evidence. The
Plaintiff chose not to call any live evidence but served 21 Civil Evidence Act notices,
intending to prove the case on documentary evidence. Those notices were served in
September 2023, but no challenge to them was made until the Defendant served his
skeleton argument on 11 January 2024.
48. Mr Harris submits that the Plaintiff won on the issue, the Notices having been
allowed/admitted; however, Mr Griffiths KC submits that the Defendant acknowledged
that the notices must be admitted, but took issue that they did not comply with the rules on
admissibility. The way they were admitted affected the evidential weight that could be
given to them. I do not think that there was anything improper in bringing the issue,
particularly in the circumstances when the notices amounted to the totality of the Plaintiff’s
evidence;19 however, Mr Harris makes the point that the challenge could have been raised
at a much earlier stage, and if it had been, the Plaintiff would have won (as the notices were
admitted) and been awarded her costs of the issue. I do not think that the argument is
unmeritorious; however, the second limb of whether I would have awarded costs if the
18 The issue at d) had been dropped in the Amended Defence.
19 No live witnesses were called by the Plaintiff.
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 15 of 20

matter had been raised earlier requires an analysis of the hypothetical. The issue of costs
on an interlocutory application of the issue would depend on several factors, for example,
as to whether the issue was raised together with other issues (Mr Harris suggests there were
approximately 7 interlocutory hearings when the issue could have been raised) and the
success or failure of the other issues and whether I would have awarded costs simply on
the issue of admissibility (which was conceded) where the notices were defective. I do not
think this raises the issue to the threshold for departing from the general principle.
49. Mr Harris also raises other issues in relation to the Defendant’s conduct of the litigation,
which he says bear on the issue of costs. Briefly, these are:
a) The challenge to Mr Harris conducting the matter;
b) The counterclaim in the action being struck out;
c) The Defendant’s decision to change representation,
which Mr Harris suggests gave rise to unreasonable delays which should be reflected in
costs. I do not agree. Agyemang CJ dealt with the challenge to Mr Harris in a ruling handed
down on 3 March 2021,20 in respect of which costs were ordered in favour of the Plaintiff.
The counterclaim was dealt with by Lobban-Jackson J in a decision handed down on 6
April 2022, and, as before, costs were awarded to the Plaintiff.
50. With respect to the change of representation, Mr Harris submits:
“… In March 2023 Griffiths & Partners came on the record. No doubt significant
costs associated with instructing new counsel and them coming up to speed were
incurred but these cannot be for the Plaintiff’s account.”
51. This is a matter for taxation and does not go to the issue of costs liability. I do not accept
what I view as an unfounded allegation by Mr Harris that the timing of the change of
counsel was part of a “strategic effort on the part of the Defendant to force the vacation of
the June trial date and delay the trial.”
52. In respect of the alleged ‘strategic effort of delay’, Mr Harris also points to the Defendant’s
applications (all of which occurred after the change of counsel):
20 Attorney General -v- Sean Sullivan (CL 53 of 2020) [2021] TCASC 6 (3 March 2021).
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 16 of 20

a) to consolidate the action with 3 other actions;21
b) to amend the Defence; and
c) for disclosure.
and submits:
“While the Defendant was entitled to change his counsel if he wished, the steps
that inevitably followed through the approach then followed should sound in
costs.”
53. The consolidation application was unsuccessful; however, the Plaintiff was awarded costs
incurred by the application. Costs issues in relation to the leave to amend the Defence and
disclosure were dealt with following the decisions. I am not of the view that it would be
correct to weigh into the consideration of the costs of the action, applications in which costs
have already been decided.
54. The final issue which Mr Harris raises is the late disclosure by the Defendant of the findings
of the Massachusetts regulator, which findings were made in 1998, upon which Mr Harris
heavily relied as proving the Defendant’s alleged bad character. Nothing in my judgment
turned on those findings. In any event, the evidence was that this issue had been presented
to the Financial Services Commission in respect of the Defendant’s application for a
financial services licence when he was employed by Atlas Securities and Temple Securities
in the country. I do not consider this an issue which reaches a threshold to have a bearing
on costs other than perhaps arguments on taxation.
Stay Pending Appeal
55. In closing, and in accepting that the Defendant is entitled to some costs, Mr Harris suggests
that the taxation of costs should be stayed pending the outcome of any appeal.22 His
argument is an obvious one: if the taxation of costs proceeds and there is a successful
appeal, then the time and costs spent on taxation would be wasted. Whilst I see the wisdom
of that argument, Mr Griffiths KC submits that there is no basis to stay the taxation. If the
21 CL 17/20 – The Hon. Attorney General -v- Kenneth Handfield; CL 18/20 - The Hon. Attorney General -v- (1)
Brandon Williams (2) Kareem Higgs; and CL 19/20 - The Hon. Attorney General -v- Anthony Steve Been.
22 Notice of Appeal was filed on 19 November 2025 and is listed for hearing on 16 to 18 March 2026.
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 17 of 20

taxation proceeds and an appeal is ultimately successful, that is a risk for the taxing party,
as they might have to refund the costs.
56. I was referred to Note 62/3/10 of the White Book, by Mr Griffiths KC, and it is based on
that Note that Mr Harris submits that it is ‘quite clear that the Court has a discretion to
grant a stay of taxation pending appeal’.
57. Note 62/3/10 provides:
“Costs to be paid under usual undertaking” – After trial of an action counsel
for the defendant may apply for a stay pending appeal. In such a case the Judge
often gives a stay for a specified number of days and if within that time the
amount awarded is paid into Court and the appeal entered execution is further
stayed until the determination of the appeal, also the taxation of costs proceeds
and the costs are paid by the defendant to the plaintiff’s solicitor on his personal
undertaking to return the same if the appeal be successful. (Emphasis added)
58. The above contemplates a decision that a payment of damages is to be made by the
defendant to the plaintiff. Mr Griffiths submits that the roles can be reversed, but I do not
think that is correct, and that the rule only applies to a successful plaintiff. If the defendant
is successful in the action, there is nothing to be paid other than costs, and even in the case
of a successful plaintiff, the rule provides that the taxation proceeds, with the costs being
held on an undertaking pending the result of the appeal, by the defendant’s attorneys.
59. I think Mr Harris' interpretation that the ‘stay’ that is being considered in the Note is a stay
of taxation is incorrect. It is a stay of the judgment pending appeal, and the rule gives the
plaintiff the comfort of knowing that he has the spoils and costs of his litigation
immediately available if a losing defendant’s appeal is unsuccessful.
60. If the Note were with reference to a stay of taxation, it would be in conflict with Ord. 62
r.3(6)(b), which provides:
(b) where this order [for costs] is made at the conclusion of a cause or matter, the
party in whose favour it is made shall be entitled to have his costs taxed forthwith;
61. I also refer to Note 62/8/1, which provides inter alia:
“… a cause or matter … is concluded when the court in question has finally
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 18 of 20

determined the matters in issue, whether or not there is an appeal from that
determination”, per Saville J. Rafsanjan Pistachio Producers Corporation v.
Bank Leumi (U.K.) Ltd November 4, 1992, Review of Taxation (unrep.)
…
An order for the taxation of costs is not normally stayed pending an appeal
provided that repayment is secured (Minnesota Mining and Manufacturing
Company v. Rennicks (UK) Ltd (No 2) [1992] R.P.C. 331, Aldous J. (Patents
Court).
The court has power to order a stay of taxation on terms, e.g. that a sum of money
be paid pending appeal (subject to terms as to repayment and interest after a
successful appeal). The court considered that this might be the most appropriate
way of dealing with cases of complex and expensive taxation of costs (or indeed
complex and expensive enquiries as to damages). The Judge held that it required
special circumstances before a stay could be granted, but that the court’s
discretion could be made conditional upon any offer that might be made by the
paying party and any condition the court might impose as a term for granting a
stay (The Lubrizol Corporation v. Esso Petroleum Company Ltd, July 15, 1997,
Jacob J. (unrep.))
62. There is no authority before me that I can grant a stay of taxation simply on the
request/submission of counsel.
Conclusion
63. The general principle is that the successful party should have their costs of the litigation
unless there is some good reason to depart from that principle. Whilst I have given careful
consideration to the issues raised by Mr Harris, I am not in agreement that the elements of
the tort should be looked at as discrete or distinct issues. There was only one claim, and
the winner was the Defendant.
64. I am also not of the view that the other matters raised were discrete or distinct issues or
were raised unreasonably, and I do not consider, applying the principles described by the
authorities, that making an issue-based costs order would, in all the circumstances of this
case, it being an outright success by a defendant, be the correct result.
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 19 of 20

65. Also, I do not see that, for the reasons above, there are any grounds for ordering a stay of
taxation.
Disposition
66. The order is therefore that the Plaintiff shall pay the Defendant’s costs of the action, to be
taxed on the standard basis if not agreed.
67. I make no order on the issue of a stay of taxation such that the same may be reconsidered
by the taxing officer, if appropriate.
19th February 2026
The Hon. Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Judge of the Supreme Court
CL-53/20 – The Honourable Attorney General of the Turks and Caicos Islands -v- Sean Sullivan (Costs)
Page 20 of 20
court: Supreme Court of Turks and Caicos Islands
summary: A wholly successful defendant is ordinarily entitled to costs; issue-based apportionment and a stay of taxation were refused.
